content.content:
1000→  1000→```
  1001→  1001→
  1002→  1002→### Notification
  1003→  1003→
  1004→  1004→Runs when Claude Code sends notifications. Matches on notification type: `permission_prompt`, `idle_prompt`, `auth_success`, `elicitation_dialog`. Omit the matcher to run hooks for all notification types.
  1005→  1005→
  1006→  1006→Use separate matchers to run different handlers depending on the notification type. This configuration triggers a permission-specific alert script when Claude needs permission approval and a different notification when Claude has been idle:
  1007→  1007→
  1008→  1008→```json  theme={null}
  1009→  1009→{
  1010→  1010→  "hooks": {
  1011→  1011→    "Notification": [
  1012→  1012→      {
  1013→  1013→        "matcher": "permission_prompt",
  1014→  1014→        "hooks": [
  1015→  1015→          {
  1016→  1016→            "type": "command",
  1017→  1017→            "command": "/path/to/permission-alert.sh"
  1018→  1018→          }
  1019→  1019→        ]
  1020→  1020→      },
  1021→  1021→      {
  1022→  1022→        "matcher": "idle_prompt",
  1023→  1023→        "hooks": [
  1024→  1024→          {
  1025→  1025→            "type": "command",
  1026→  1026→            "command": "/path/to/idle-notification.sh"
  1027→  1027→          }
  1028→  1028→        ]
  1029→  1029→      }
  1030→  1030→    ]
  1031→  1031→  }
  1032→  1032→}
  1033→  1033→```
  1034→  1034→
  1035→  1035→#### Notification input
  1036→  1036→
  1037→  1037→In addition to the [common input fields](#common-input-fields), Notification hooks receive `message` with the notification text, an optional `title`, and `notification_type` indicating which type fired.
  1038→  1038→
  1039→  1039→```json  theme={null}
  1040→  1040→{
  1041→  1041→  "session_id": "abc123",
  1042→  1042→  "transcript_path": "/Users/.../.claude/projects/.../00893aaf-19fa-41d2-8238-13269b9b3ca0.jsonl",
  1043→  1043→  "cwd": "/Users/...",
  1044→  1044→  "permission_mode": "default",
  1045→  1045→  "hook_event_name": "Notification",
  1046→  1046→  "message": "Claude needs your permission to use Bash",
  1047→  1047→  "title": "Permission needed",
  1048→  1048→  "notification_type": "permission_prompt"
  1049→  1049→}
  1050→  1050→```
  1051→  1051→
  1052→  1052→Notification hooks cannot block or modify notifications. In addition to the [JSON output fields](#json-output) available to all hooks, you can return `additionalContext` to add context to the conversation:
  1053→  1053→
  1054→  1054→| Field               | Description                      |
  1055→  1055→| :------------------ | :------------------------------- |
  1056→  1056→| `additionalContext` | String added to Claude's context |
  1057→  1057→
  1058→  1058→### SubagentStart
  1059→  1059→
  1060→  1060→Runs when a Claude Code subagent is spawned via the Task tool. Supports matchers to filter by agent type name (built-in agents like `Bash`, `Explore`, `Plan`, or custom agent names from `.claude/agents/`).
  1061→  1061→
  1062→  1062→#### SubagentStart input
  1063→  1063→
  1064→  1064→In addition to the [common input fields](#common-input-fields), SubagentStart hooks receive `agent_id` with the unique identifier for the subagent and `agent_type` with the agent name (built-in agents like `"Bash"`, `"Explore"`, `"Plan"`, or custom agent names).
  1065→  1065→
  1066→  1066→```json  theme={null}
  1067→  1067→{
  1068→  1068→  "session_id": "abc123",
  1069→  1069→  "transcript_path": "/Users/.../.claude/projects/.../00893aaf-19fa-41d2-8238-13269b9b3ca0.jsonl",
  1070→  1070→  "cwd": "/Users/...",
  1071→  1071→  "permission_mode": "default",
  1072→  1072→  "hook_event_name": "SubagentStart",
  1073→  1073→  "agent_id": "agent-abc123",
  1074→  1074→  "agent_type": "Explore"
  1075→  1075→}
  1076→  1076→```
  1077→  1077→
  1078→  1078→SubagentStart hooks cannot block subagent creation, but they can inject context into the subagent. In addition to the [JSON output fields](#json-output) available to all hooks, you can return:
  1079→  1079→
  1080→  1080→| Field               | Description                            |
  1081→  1081→| :------------------ | :------------------------------------- |
  1082→  1082→| `additionalContext` | String added to the subagent's context |
  1083→  1083→
  1084→  1084→```json  theme={null}
  1085→  1085→{
  1086→  1086→  "hookSpecificOutput": {
  1087→  1087→    "hookEventName": "SubagentStart",
  1088→  1088→    "additionalContext": "Follow security guidelines for this task"
  1089→  1089→  }
  1090→  1090→}
  1091→  1091→```
  1092→  1092→
  1093→  1093→### SubagentStop
  1094→  1094→
  1095→  1095→Runs when a Claude Code subagent has finished responding. Matches on agent type, same values as SubagentStart.
  1096→  1096→
  1097→  1097→#### SubagentStop input
  1098→  1098→
  1099→  1099→In addition to the [common input fields](#common-input-fields), SubagentStop hooks receive `stop_hook_active`, `agent_id`, `agent_type`, `agent_transcript_path`, and `last_assistant_message`. The `agent_type` field is the value used for matcher filtering. The `transcript_path` is the main session's transcript, while `agent_transcript_path` is the subagent's own transcript stored in a nested `subagents/` folder. The `last_assistant_message` field contains the text content of the subagent's final response, so hooks can access it without parsing the transcript file.
  1100→  1100→
  1101→  1101→```json  theme={null}
  1102→  1102→{
  1103→  1103→  "session_id": "abc123",
  1104→  1104→  "transcript_path": "~/.claude/projects/.../abc123.jsonl",
  1105→  1105→  "cwd": "/Users/...",
  1106→  1106→  "permission_mode": "default",
  1107→  1107→  "hook_event_name": "SubagentStop",
  1108→  1108→  "stop_hook_active": false,
  1109→  1109→  "agent_id": "def456",
  1110→  1110→  "agent_type": "Explore",
  1111→  1111→  "agent_transcript_path": "~/.claude/projects/.../abc123/subagents/agent-def456.jsonl",
  1112→  1112→  "last_assistant_message": "Analysis complete. Found 3 potential issues..."
  1113→  1113→}
  1114→  1114→```
  1115→  1115→
  1116→  1116→SubagentStop hooks use the same decision control format as [Stop hooks](#stop-decision-control).
  1117→  1117→
  1118→  1118→### Stop
  1119→  1119→
  1120→  1120→Runs when the main Claude Code agent has finished responding. Does not run if
  1121→  1121→the stoppage occurred due to a user interrupt.
  1122→  1122→
  1123→  1123→#### Stop input
  1124→  1124→
  1125→  1125→In addition to the [common input fields](#common-input-fields), Stop hooks receive `stop_hook_active` and `last_assistant_message`. The `stop_hook_active` field is `true` when Claude Code is already continuing as a result of a stop hook. Check this value or process the transcript to prevent Claude Code from running indefinitely. The `last_assistant_message` field contains the text content of Claude's final response, so hooks can access it without parsing the transcript file.
  1126→  1126→
  1127→  1127→```json  theme={null}
  1128→  1128→{
  1129→  1129→  "session_id": "abc123",
  1130→  1130→  "transcript_path": "~/.claude/projects/.../00893aaf-19fa-41d2-8238-13269b9b3ca0.jsonl",
  1131→  1131→  "cwd": "/Users/...",
  1132→  1132→  "permission_mode": "default",
  1133→  1133→  "hook_event_name": "Stop",
  1134→  1134→  "stop_hook_active": true,
  1135→  1135→  "last_assistant_message": "I've completed the refactoring. Here's a summary..."
  1136→  1136→}
  1137→  1137→```
  1138→  1138→
  1139→  1139→#### Stop decision control
  1140→  1140→
  1141→  1141→`Stop` and `SubagentStop` hooks can control whether Claude continues. In addition to the [JSON output fields](#json-output) available to all hooks, your hook script can return these event-specific fields:
  1142→  1142→
  1143→  1143→| Field      | Description                                                                |
  1144→  1144→| :--------- | :------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
  1145→  1145→| `decision` | `"block"` prevents Claude from stopping. Omit to allow Claude to stop      |
  1146→  1146→| `reason`   | Required when `decision` is `"block"`. Tells Claude why it should continue |
  1147→  1147→
  1148→  1148→```json  theme={null}
  1149→  1149→{
  1150→  1150→  "decision": "block",
  1151→  1151→  "reason": "Must be provided when Claude is blocked from stopping"
  1152→  1152→}
  1153→  1153→```
  1154→  1154→
  1155→  1155→### TeammateIdle
  1156→  1156→
  1157→  1157→Runs when an [agent team](/en/agent-teams) teammate is about to go idle after finishing its turn. Use this to enforce quality gates before a teammate stops working, such as requiring passing lint checks or verifying that output files exist.
  1158→  1158→
  1159→  1159→When a `TeammateIdle` hook exits with code 2, the teammate receives the stderr message as feedback and continues working instead of going idle. TeammateIdle hooks do not support matchers and fire on every occurrence.
  1160→  1160→
  1161→  1161→#### TeammateIdle input
  1162→  1162→
  1163→  1163→In addition to the [common input fields](#common-input-fields), TeammateIdle hooks receive `teammate_name` and `team_name`.
  1164→  1164→
  1165→  1165→```json  theme={null}
  1166→  1166→{
  1167→  1167→  "session_id": "abc123",
  1168→  1168→  "transcript_path": "/Users/.../.claude/projects/.../00893aaf-19fa-41d2-8238-13269b9b3ca0.jsonl",
  1169→  1169→  "cwd": "/Users/...",
  1170→  1170→  "permission_mode": "default",
  1171→  1171→  "hook_event_name": "TeammateIdle",
  1172→  1172→  "teammate_name": "researcher",
  1173→  1173→  "team_name": "my-project"
  1174→  1174→}
  1175→  1175→```
  1176→  1176→
  1177→  1177→| Field           | Description                                   |
  1178→  1178→| :-------------- | :-------------------------------------------- |
  1179→  1179→| `teammate_name` | Name of the teammate that is about to go idle |
  1180→  1180→| `team_name`     | Name of the team                              |
  1181→  1181→
  1182→  1182→#### TeammateIdle decision control
  1183→  1183→
  1184→  1184→TeammateIdle hooks use exit codes only, not JSON decision control. This example checks that a build artifact exists before allowing a teammate to go idle:
  1185→  1185→
  1186→  1186→```bash  theme={null}
  1187→  1187→#!/bin/bash
  1188→  1188→
  1189→  1189→if [ ! -f "./dist/output.js" ]; then
  1190→  1190→  echo "Build artifact missing. Run the build before stopping." >&2
  1191→  1191→  exit 2
  1192→  1192→fi
  1193→  1193→
  1194→  1194→exit 0
  1195→  1195→```
  1196→  1196→
  1197→  1197→### TaskCompleted
  1198→  1198→
  1199→  1199→Runs when a task is being marked as completed. This fires in two situations: when any agent explicitly marks a task as completed through the TaskUpdate tool, or when an [agent team](/en/agent-teams) teammate finishes its turn with in-progress tasks. Use this to enforce completion criteria like passing tests or lint checks before a task can close.
  1200→  1200→
  1201→  1201→When a `TaskCompleted` hook exits with code 2, the task is not marked as completed and the stderr message is fed back to the model as feedback. TaskCompleted hooks do not support matchers and fire on every occurrence.
  1202→  1202→
  1203→  1203→#### TaskCompleted input
  1204→  1204→
  1205→  1205→In addition to the [common input fields](#common-input-fields), TaskCompleted hooks receive `task_id`, `task_subject`, and optionally `task_description`, `teammate_name`, and `team_name`.
  1206→  1206→
  1207→  1207→```json  theme={null}
  1208→  1208→{
  1209→  1209→  "session_id": "abc123",
  1210→  1210→  "transcript_path": "/Users/.../.claude/projects/.../00893aaf-19fa-41d2-8238-13269b9b3ca0.jsonl",
  1211→  1211→  "cwd": "/Users/...",
  1212→  1212→  "permission_mode": "default",
  1213→  1213→  "hook_event_name": "TaskCompleted",
  1214→  1214→  "task_id": "task-001",
  1215→  1215→  "task_subject": "Implement user authentication",
  1216→  1216→  "task_description": "Add login and signup endpoints",
  1217→  1217→  "teammate_name": "implementer",
  1218→  1218→  "team_name": "my-project"
  1219→  1219→}
  1220→  1220→```
  1221→  1221→
  1222→  1222→| Field              | Description                                             |
  1223→  1223→| :----------------- | :------------------------------------------------------ |
  1224→  1224→| `task_id`          | Identifier of the task being completed                  |
  1225→  1225→| `task_subject`     | Title of the task                                       |
  1226→  1226→| `task_description` | Detailed description of the task. May be absent         |
  1227→  1227→| `teammate_name`    | Name of the teammate completing the task. May be absent |
  1228→  1228→| `team_name`        | Name of the team. May be absent                         |
  1229→  1229→
  1230→  1230→#### TaskCompleted decision control
  1231→  1231→
  1232→  1232→TaskCompleted hooks use exit codes only, not JSON decision control. This example runs tests and blocks task completion if they fail:
  1233→  1233→
  1234→  1234→```bash  theme={null}
  1235→  1235→#!/bin/bash
  1236→  1236→INPUT=$(cat)
  1237→  1237→TASK_SUBJECT=$(echo "$INPUT" | jq -r '.task_subject')
  1238→  1238→
  1239→  1239→# Run the test suite
  1240→  1240→if ! npm test 2>&1; then
  1241→  1241→  echo "Tests not passing. Fix failing tests before completing: $TASK_SUBJECT" >&2
  1242→  1242→  exit 2
  1243→  1243→fi
  1244→  1244→
  1245→  1245→exit 0
  1246→  1246→```
  1247→  1247→
  1248→  1248→### ConfigChange
  1249→  1249→
  1250→  1250→Runs when a configuration file changes during a session. Use this to audit settings changes, enforce security policies, or block unauthorized modifications to configuration files.
  1251→  1251→
  1252→  1252→ConfigChange hooks fire for changes to settings files, managed policy settings, and skill files. The `source` field in the input tells you which type of configuration changed, and the optional `file_path` field provides the path to the changed file.
  1253→  1253→
  1254→  1254→The matcher filters on the configuration source:
  1255→  1255→
  1256→  1256→| Matcher            | When it fires                             |
  1257→  1257→| :----------------- | :---------------------------------------- |
  1258→  1258→| `user_settings`    | `~/.claude/settings.json` changes         |
  1259→  1259→| `project_settings` | `.claude/settings.json` changes           |
  1260→  1260→| `local_settings`   | `.claude/settings.local.json` changes     |
  1261→  1261→| `policy_settings`  | Managed policy settings change            |
  1262→  1262→| `skills`           | A skill file in `.claude/skills/` changes |
  1263→  1263→
  1264→  1264→This example logs all configuration changes for security auditing:
  1265→  1265→
  1266→  1266→```json  theme={null}
  1267→  1267→{
  1268→  1268→  "hooks": {
  1269→  1269→    "ConfigChange": [
  1270→  1270→      {
  1271→  1271→        "hooks": [
  1272→  1272→          {
  1273→  1273→            "type": "command",
  1274→  1274→            "command": "\"$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR\"/.claude/hooks/audit-config-change.sh"
  1275→  1275→          }
  1276→  1276→        ]
  1277→  1277→      }
  1278→  1278→    ]
  1279→  1279→  }
  1280→  1280→}
  1281→  1281→```
  1282→  1282→
  1283→  1283→#### ConfigChange input
  1284→  1284→
  1285→  1285→In addition to the [common input fields](#common-input-fields), ConfigChange hooks receive `source` and optionally `file_path`. The `source` field indicates which configuration type changed, and `file_path` provides the path to the specific file that was modified.
  1286→  1286→
  1287→  1287→```json  theme={null}
  1288→  1288→{
  1289→  1289→  "session_id": "abc123",
  1290→  1290→  "transcript_path": "/Users/.../.claude/projects/.../00893aaf-19fa-41d2-8238-13269b9b3ca0.jsonl",
  1291→  1291→  "cwd": "/Users/...",
  1292→  1292→  "permission_mode": "default",
  1293→  1293→  "hook_event_name": "ConfigChange",
  1294→  1294→  "source": "project_settings",
  1295→  1295→  "file_path": "/Users/.../my-project/.claude/settings.json"
  1296→  1296→}
  1297→  1297→```
  1298→  1298→
  1299→  1299→#### ConfigChange decision control
  1300→  1300→
  1301→  1301→ConfigChange hooks can block configuration changes from taking effect. Use exit code 2 or a JSON `decision` to prevent the change. When blocked, the new settings are not applied to the running session.
  1302→  1302→
  1303→  1303→| Field      | Description                                                                              |
  1304→  1304→| :--------- | :--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
  1305→  1305→| `decision` | `"block"` prevents the configuration change from being applied. Omit to allow the change |
  1306→  1306→| `reason`   | Explanation shown to the user when `decision` is `"block"`                               |
  1307→  1307→
  1308→  1308→```json  theme={null}
  1309→  1309→{
  1310→  1310→  "decision": "block",
  1311→  1311→  "reason": "Configuration changes to project settings require admin approval"
  1312→  1312→}
  1313→  1313→```
  1314→  1314→
  1315→  1315→`policy_settings` changes cannot be blocked. Hooks still fire for `policy_settings` sources, so you can use them for audit logging, but any blocking decision is ignored. This ensures enterprise-managed settings always take effect.
  1316→  1316→
  1317→  1317→### WorktreeCreate
  1318→  1318→
  1319→  1319→When you run `claude --worktree` or a [subagent uses `isolation: "worktree"`](/en/sub-agents#choose-the-subagent-scope), Claude Code creates an isolated working copy using `git worktree`. If you configure a WorktreeCreate hook, it replaces the default git behavior, letting you use a different version control system like SVN, Perforce, or Mercurial.
  1320→  1320→
  1321→  1321→The hook must print the absolute path to the created worktree directory on stdout. Claude Code uses this path as the working directory for the isolated session.
  1322→  1322→
  1323→  1323→This example creates an SVN working copy and prints the path for Claude Code to use. Replace the repository URL with your own:
  1324→  1324→
  1325→  1325→```json  theme={null}
  1326→  1326→{
  1327→  1327→  "hooks": {
  1328→  1328→    "WorktreeCreate": [
  1329→  1329→      {
  1330→  1330→        "hooks": [
  1331→  1331→          {
  1332→  1332→            "type": "command",
  1333→  1333→            "command": "bash -c 'NAME=$(jq -r .name); DIR=\"$HOME/.claude/worktrees/$NAME\"; svn checkout https://svn.example.com/repo/trunk \"$DIR\" >&2 && echo \"$DIR\"'"
  1334→  1334→          }
  1335→  1335→        ]
  1336→  1336→      }
  1337→  1337→    ]
  1338→  1338→  }
  1339→  1339→}
  1340→  1340→```
  1341→  1341→
  1342→  1342→The hook reads the worktree `name` from the JSON input on stdin, checks out a fresh copy into a new directory, and prints the directory path. The `echo` on the last line is what Claude Code reads as the worktree path. Redirect any other output to stderr so it doesn't interfere with the path.
  1343→  1343→
  1344→  1344→#### WorktreeCreate input
  1345→  1345→
  1346→  1346→In addition to the [common input fields](#common-input-fields), WorktreeCreate hooks receive the `name` field. This is a slug identifier for the new worktree, either specified by the user or auto-generated (for example, `bold-oak-a3f2`).
  1347→  1347→
  1348→  1348→```json  theme={null}
  1349→  1349→{
  1350→  1350→  "session_id": "abc123",
  1351→  1351→  "transcript_path": "/Users/.../.claude/projects/.../00893aaf-19fa-41d2-8238-13269b9b3ca0.jsonl",
  1352→  1352→  "cwd": "/Users/...",
  1353→  1353→  "hook_event_name": "WorktreeCreate",
  1354→  1354→  "name": "feature-auth"
  1355→  1355→}
  1356→  1356→```
  1357→  1357→
  1358→  1358→#### WorktreeCreate output
  1359→  1359→
  1360→  1360→The hook must print the absolute path to the created worktree directory on stdout. If the hook fails or produces no output, worktree creation fails with an error.
  1361→  1361→
  1362→  1362→WorktreeCreate hooks do not use the standard allow/block decision model. Instead, the hook's success or failure determines the outcome. Only `type: "command"` hooks are supported.
  1363→  1363→
  1364→  1364→### WorktreeRemove
  1365→  1365→
  1366→  1366→The cleanup counterpart to [WorktreeCreate](#worktreecreate). This hook fires when a worktree is being removed, either when you exit a `--worktree` session and choose to remove it, or when a subagent with `isolation: "worktree"` finishes. For git-based worktrees, Claude handles cleanup automatically with `git worktree remove`. If you configured a WorktreeCreate hook for a non-git version control system, pair it with a WorktreeRemove hook to handle cleanup. Without one, the worktree directory is left on disk.
  1367→  1367→
  1368→  1368→Claude Code passes the path that WorktreeCreate printed on stdout as `worktree_path` in the hook input. This example reads that path and removes the directory:
  1369→  1369→
  1370→  1370→```json  theme={null}
  1371→  1371→{
  1372→  1372→  "hooks": {
  1373→  1373→    "WorktreeRemove": [
  1374→  1374→      {
  1375→  1375→        "hooks": [
  1376→  1376→          {
  1377→  1377→            "type": "command",
  1378→  1378→            "command": "bash -c 'jq -r .worktree_path | xargs rm -rf'"
  1379→  1379→          }
  1380→  1380→        ]
  1381→  1381→      }
  1382→  1382→    ]
  1383→  1383→  }
  1384→  1384→}
  1385→  1385→```
  1386→  1386→
  1387→  1387→#### WorktreeRemove input
  1388→  1388→
  1389→  1389→In addition to the [common input fields](#common-input-fields), WorktreeRemove hooks receive the `worktree_path` field, which is the absolute path to the worktree being removed.
  1390→  1390→
  1391→  1391→```json  theme={null}
  1392→  1392→{
  1393→  1393→  "session_id": "abc123",
  1394→  1394→  "transcript_path": "/Users/.../.claude/projects/.../00893aaf-19fa-41d2-8238-13269b9b3ca0.jsonl",
  1395→  1395→  "cwd": "/Users/...",
  1396→  1396→  "hook_event_name": "WorktreeRemove",
  1397→  1397→  "worktree_path": "/Users/.../my-project/.claude/worktrees/feature-auth"
  1398→  1398→}
  1399→  1399→```
  1400→  1400→
  1401→  1401→WorktreeRemove hooks have no decision control. They cannot block worktree removal but can perform cleanup tasks like removing version control state or archiving changes. Hook failures are logged in debug mode only. Only `type: "command"` hooks are supported.
  1402→  1402→
  1403→  1403→### PreCompact
  1404→  1404→
  1405→  1405→Runs before Claude Code is about to run a compact operation.
  1406→  1406→
  1407→  1407→The matcher value indicates whether compaction was triggered manually or automatically:
  1408→  1408→
  1409→  1409→| Matcher  | When it fires                                |
  1410→  1410→| :------- | :------------------------------------------- |
  1411→  1411→| `manual` | `/compact`                                   |
  1412→  1412→| `auto`   | Auto-compact when the context window is full |
  1413→  1413→
  1414→  1414→#### PreCompact input
  1415→  1415→
  1416→  1416→In addition to the [common input fields](#common-input-fields), PreCompact hooks receive `trigger` and `custom_instructions`. For `manual`, `custom_instructions` contains what the user passes into `/compact`. For `auto`, `custom_instructions` is empty.
  1417→  1417→
  1418→  1418→```json  theme={null}
  1419→  1419→{
  1420→  1420→  "session_id": "abc123",
  1421→  1421→  "transcript_path": "/Users/.../.claude/projects/.../00893aaf-19fa-41d2-8238-13269b9b3ca0.jsonl",
  1422→  1422→  "cwd": "/Users/...",
  1423→  1423→  "permission_mode": "default",
  1424→  1424→  "hook_event_name": "PreCompact",
  1425→  1425→  "trigger": "manual",
  1426→  1426→  "custom_instructions": ""
  1427→  1427→}
  1428→  1428→```
  1429→  1429→
  1430→  1430→### SessionEnd
  1431→  1431→
  1432→  1432→Runs when a Claude Code session ends. Useful for cleanup tasks, logging session
  1433→  1433→statistics, or saving session state. Supports matchers to filter by exit reason.
  1434→  1434→
  1435→  1435→The `reason` field in the hook input indicates why the session ended:
  1436→  1436→
  1437→  1437→| Reason                        | Description                                |
  1438→  1438→| :---------------------------- | :----------------------------------------- |
  1439→  1439→| `clear`                       | Session cleared with `/clear` command      |
  1440→  1440→| `logout`                      | User logged out                            |
  1441→  1441→| `prompt_input_exit`           | User exited while prompt input was visible |
  1442→  1442→| `bypass_permissions_disabled` | Bypass permissions mode was disabled       |
  1443→  1443→| `other`                       | Other exit reasons                         |
  1444→  1444→
  1445→  1445→#### SessionEnd input
  1446→  1446→
  1447→  1447→In addition to the [common input fields](#common-input-fields), SessionEnd hooks receive a `reason` field indicating why the session ended. See the [reason table](#sessionend) above for all values.
  1448→  1448→
  1449→  1449→```json  theme={null}
  1450→  1450→{
  1451→  1451→  "session_id": "abc123",
  1452→  1452→  "transcript_path": "/Users/.../.claude/projects/.../00893aaf-19fa-41d2-8238-13269b9b3ca0.jsonl",
  1453→  1453→  "cwd": "/Users/...",
  1454→  1454→  "permission_mode": "default",
  1455→  1455→  "hook_event_name": "SessionEnd",
  1456→  1456→  "reason": "other"
  1457→  1457→}
  1458→  1458→```
  1459→  1459→
  1460→  1460→SessionEnd hooks have no decision control. They cannot block session termination but can perform cleanup tasks.
  1461→  1461→
  1462→  1462→## Prompt-based hooks
  1463→  1463→
  1464→  1464→In addition to Bash command hooks (`type: "command"`), Claude Code supports prompt-based hooks (`type: "prompt"`) that use an LLM to evaluate whether to allow or block an action, and agent hooks (`type: "agent"`) that spawn an agentic verifier with tool access. Not all events support every hook type.
  1465→  1465→
  1466→  1466→Events that support all three hook types (`command`, `prompt`, and `agent`):
  1467→  1467→
  1468→  1468→* `PermissionRequest`
  1469→  1469→* `PostToolUse`
  1470→  1470→* `PostToolUseFailure`
  1471→  1471→* `PreToolUse`
  1472→  1472→* `Stop`
  1473→  1473→* `SubagentStop`
  1474→  1474→* `TaskCompleted`
  1475→  1475→* `UserPromptSubmit`
  1476→  1476→
  1477→  1477→Events that only support `type: "command"` hooks:
  1478→  1478→
  1479→  1479→* `ConfigChange`
  1480→  1480→* `Notification`
  1481→  1481→* `PreCompact`
  1482→  1482→* `SessionEnd`
  1483→  1483→* `SessionStart`
  1484→  1484→* `SubagentStart`
  1485→  1485→* `TeammateIdle`
  1486→  1486→* `WorktreeCreate`
  1487→  1487→* `WorktreeRemove`
  1488→  1488→
  1489→  1489→### How prompt-based hooks work
  1490→  1490→
  1491→  1491→Instead of executing a Bash command, prompt-based hooks:
  1492→  1492→
  1493→  1493→1. Send the hook input and your prompt to a Claude model, Haiku by default
  1494→  1494→2. The LLM responds with structured JSON containing a decision
  1495→  1495→3. Claude Code processes the decision automatically
  1496→  1496→
  1497→  1497→### Prompt hook configuration
  1498→  1498→
  1499→  1499→Set `type` to `"prompt"` and provide a `prompt` string instead of a `command`. Use the `$ARGUMENTS` placeholder to inject the hook's JSON input data into your prompt text. Claude Code sends the combined prompt and input to a fast Claude model, which returns a JSON decision.
  1500→  1500→
  1501→  1501→This `Stop` hook asks the LLM to evaluate whether all tasks are complete before allowing Claude to finish:
  1502→  1502→
  1503→  1503→```json  theme={null}
  1504→  1504→{
  1505→  1505→  "hooks": {
  1506→  1506→    "Stop": [
  1507→  1507→      {
  1508→  1508→        "hooks": [
  1509→  1509→          {
  1510→  1510→            "type": "prompt",
  1511→  1511→            "prompt": "Evaluate if Claude should stop: $ARGUMENTS. Check if all tasks are complete."
  1512→  1512→          }
  1513→  1513→        ]
  1514→  1514→      }
  1515→  1515→    ]
  1516→  1516→  }
  1517→  1517→}
  1518→  1518→```
  1519→  1519→
  1520→  1520→| Field     | Required | Description                                                                                                                                                         |
  1521→  1521→| :-------- | :------- | :------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
  1522→  1522→| `type`    | yes      | Must be `"prompt"`                                                                                                                                                  |
  1523→  1523→| `prompt`  | yes      | The prompt text to send to the LLM. Use `$ARGUMENTS` as a placeholder for the hook input JSON. If `$ARGUMENTS` is not present, input JSON is appended to the prompt |
  1524→  1524→| `model`   | no       | Model to use for evaluation. Defaults to a fast model                                                                                                               |
  1525→  1525→| `timeout` | no       | Timeout in seconds. Default: 30                                                                                                                                     |
  1526→  1526→
  1527→  1527→### Response schema
  1528→  1528→
  1529→  1529→The LLM must respond with JSON containing:
  1530→  1530→
  1531→  1531→```json  theme={null}
  1532→  1532→{
  1533→  1533→  "ok": true | false,
  1534→  1534→  "reason": "Explanation for the decision"
  1535→  1535→}
  1536→  1536→```
  1537→  1537→
  1538→  1538→| Field    | Description                                                |
  1539→  1539→| :------- | :--------------------------------------------------------- |
  1540→  1540→| `ok`     | `true` allows the action, `false` prevents it              |
  1541→  1541→| `reason` | Required when `ok` is `false`. Explanation shown to Claude |
  1542→  1542→
  1543→  1543→### Example: Multi-criteria Stop hook
  1544→  1544→
  1545→  1545→This `Stop` hook uses a detailed prompt to check three conditions before allowing Claude to stop. If `"ok"` is `false`, Claude continues working with the provided reason as its next instruction. `SubagentStop` hooks use the same format to evaluate whether a [subagent](/en/sub-agents) should stop:
  1546→  1546→
  1547→  1547→```json  theme={null}
  1548→  1548→{
  1549→  1549→  "hooks": {
  1550→  1550→    "Stop": [
  1551→  1551→      {
  1552→  1552→        "hooks": [
  1553→  1553→          {
  1554→  1554→            "type": "prompt",
  1555→  1555→            "prompt": "You are evaluating whether Claude should stop working. Context: $ARGUMENTS\n\nAnalyze the conversation and determine if:\n1. All user-requested tasks are complete\n2. Any errors need to be addressed\n3. Follow-up work is needed\n\nRespond with JSON: {\"ok\": true} to allow stopping, or {\"ok\": false, \"reason\": \"your explanation\"} to continue working.",
  1556→  1556→            "timeout": 30
  1557→  1557→          }
  1558→  1558→        ]
  1559→  1559→      }
  1560→  1560→    ]
  1561→  1561→  }
  1562→  1562→}
  1563→  1563→```
  1564→  1564→
  1565→  1565→## Agent-based hooks
  1566→  1566→
  1567→  1567→Agent-based hooks (`type: "agent"`) are like prompt-based hooks but with multi-turn tool access. Instead of a single LLM call, an agent hook spawns a subagent that can read files, search code, and inspect the codebase to verify conditions. Agent hooks support the same events as prompt-based hooks.
  1568→  1568→
  1569→  1569→### How agent hooks work
  1570→  1570→
  1571→  1571→When an agent hook fires:
  1572→  1572→
  1573→  1573→1. Claude Code spawns a subagent with your prompt and the hook's JSON input
  1574→  1574→2. The subagent can use tools like Read, Grep, and Glob to investigate
  1575→  1575→3. After up to 50 turns, the subagent returns a structured `{ "ok": true/false }` decision
  1576→  1576→4. Claude Code processes the decision the same way as a prompt hook
  1577→  1577→
  1578→  1578→Agent hooks are useful when verification requires inspecting actual files or test output, not just evaluating the hook input data alone.
  1579→  1579→
  1580→  1580→### Agent hook configuration
  1581→  1581→
  1582→  1582→Set `type` to `"agent"` and provide a `prompt` string. The configuration fields are the same as [prompt hooks](#prompt-hook-configuration), with a longer default timeout:
  1583→  1583→
  1584→  1584→| Field     | Required | Description                                                                                 |
  1585→  1585→| :-------- | :------- | :------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
  1586→  1586→| `type`    | yes      | Must be `"agent"`                                                                           |
  1587→  1587→| `prompt`  | yes      | Prompt describing what to verify. Use `$ARGUMENTS` as a placeholder for the hook input JSON |
  1588→  1588→| `model`   | no       | Model to use. Defaults to a fast model                                                      |
  1589→  1589→| `timeout` | no       | Timeout in seconds. Default: 60                                                             |
  1590→  1590→
  1591→  1591→The response schema is the same as prompt hooks: `{ "ok": true }` to allow or `{ "ok": false, "reason": "..." }` to block.
  1592→  1592→
  1593→  1593→This `Stop` hook verifies that all unit tests pass before allowing Claude to finish:
  1594→  1594→
  1595→  1595→```json  theme={null}
  1596→  1596→{
  1597→  1597→  "hooks": {
  1598→  1598→    "Stop": [
  1599→  1599→      {
  1600→  1600→        "hooks": [
  1601→  1601→          {
  1602→  1602→            "type": "agent",
  1603→  1603→            "prompt": "Verify that all unit tests pass. Run the test suite and check the results. $ARGUMENTS",
  1604→  1604→            "timeout": 120
  1605→  1605→          }
  1606→  1606→        ]
  1607→  1607→      }
  1608→  1608→    ]
  1609→  1609→  }
  1610→  1610→}
  1611→  1611→```
  1612→  1612→
  1613→  1613→## Run hooks in the background
  1614→  1614→
  1615→  1615→By default, hooks block Claude's execution until they complete. For long-running tasks like deployments, test suites, or external API calls, set `"async": true` to run the hook in the background while Claude continues working. Async hooks cannot block or control Claude's behavior: response fields like `decision`, `permissionDecision`, and `continue` have no effect, because the action they would have controlled has already completed.
  1616→  1616→
  1617→  1617→### Configure an async hook
  1618→  1618→
  1619→  1619→Add `"async": true` to a command hook's configuration to run it in the background without blocking Claude. This field is only available on `type: "command"` hooks.
  1620→  1620→
  1621→  1621→This hook runs a test script after every `Write` tool call. Claude continues working immediately while `run-tests.sh` executes for up to 120 seconds. When the script finishes, its output is delivered on the next conversation turn:
  1622→  1622→
  1623→  1623→```json  theme={null}
  1624→  1624→{
  1625→  1625→  "hooks": {
  1626→  1626→    "PostToolUse": [
  1627→  1627→      {
  1628→  1628→        "matcher": "Write",
  1629→  1629→        "hooks": [
  1630→  1630→          {
  1631→  1631→            "type": "command",
  1632→  1632→            "command": "/path/to/run-tests.sh",
  1633→  1633→            "async": true,
  1634→  1634→            "timeout": 120
  1635→  1635→          }
  1636→  1636→        ]
  1637→  1637→      }
  1638→  1638→    ]
  1639→  1639→  }
  1640→  1640→}
  1641→  1641→```
  1642→  1642→
  1643→  1643→The `timeout` field sets the maximum time in seconds for the background process. If not specified, async hooks use the same 10-minute default as sync hooks.
  1644→  1644→
  1645→  1645→### How async hooks execute
  1646→  1646→
  1647→  1647→When an async hook fires, Claude Code starts the hook process and immediately continues without waiting for it to finish. The hook receives the same JSON input via stdin as a synchronous hook.
  1648→  1648→
  1649→  1649→After the background process exits, if the hook produced a JSON response with a `systemMessage` or `additionalContext` field, that content is delivered to Claude as context on the next conversation turn.
  1650→  1650→
  1651→  1651→### Example: run tests after file changes
  1652→  1652→
  1653→  1653→This hook starts a test suite in the background whenever Claude writes a file, then reports the results back to Claude when the tests finish. Save this script to `.claude/hooks/run-tests-async.sh` in your project and make it executable with `chmod +x`:
  1654→  1654→
  1655→  1655→```bash  theme={null}
  1656→  1656→#!/bin/bash
  1657→  1657→# run-tests-async.sh
  1658→  1658→
  1659→  1659→# Read hook input from stdin
  1660→  1660→INPUT=$(cat)
  1661→  1661→FILE_PATH=$(echo "$INPUT" | jq -r '.tool_input.file_path // empty')
  1662→  1662→
  1663→  1663→# Only run tests for source files
  1664→  1664→if [[ "$FILE_PATH" != *.ts && "$FILE_PATH" != *.js ]]; then
  1665→  1665→  exit 0
  1666→  1666→fi
  1667→  1667→
  1668→  1668→# Run tests and report results via systemMessage
  1669→  1669→RESULT=$(npm test 2>&1)
  1670→  1670→EXIT_CODE=$?
  1671→  1671→
  1672→  1672→if [ $EXIT_CODE -eq 0 ]; then
  1673→  1673→  echo "{\"systemMessage\": \"Tests passed after editing $FILE_PATH\"}"
  1674→  1674→else
  1675→  1675→  echo "{\"systemMessage\": \"Tests failed after editing $FILE_PATH: $RESULT\"}"
  1676→  1676→fi
  1677→  1677→```
  1678→  1678→
  1679→  1679→Then add this configuration to `.claude/settings.json` in your project root. The `async: true` flag lets Claude keep working while tests run:
  1680→  1680→
  1681→  1681→```json  theme={null}
  1682→  1682→{
  1683→  1683→  "hooks": {
  1684→  1684→    "PostToolUse": [
  1685→  1685→      {
  1686→  1686→        "matcher": "Write|Edit",
  1687→  1687→        "hooks": [
  1688→  1688→          {
  1689→  1689→            "type": "command",
  1690→  1690→            "command": "\"$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR\"/.claude/hooks/run-tests-async.sh",
  1691→  1691→            "async": true,
  1692→  1692→            "timeout": 300
  1693→  1693→          }
  1694→  1694→        ]
  1695→  1695→      }
  1696→  1696→    ]
  1697→  1697→  }
  1698→  1698→}
  1699→  1699→```
  1700→  1700→
  1701→  1701→### Limitations
  1702→  1702→
  1703→  1703→Async hooks have several constraints compared to synchronous hooks:
  1704→  1704→
  1705→  1705→* Only `type: "command"` hooks support `async`. Prompt-based hooks cannot run asynchronously.
  1706→  1706→* Async hooks cannot block tool calls or return decisions. By the time the hook completes, the triggering action has already proceeded.
  1707→  1707→* Hook output is delivered on the next conversation turn. If the session is idle, the response waits until the next user interaction.
  1708→  1708→* Each execution creates a separate background process. There is no deduplication across multiple firings of the same async hook.
  1709→  1709→
  1710→  1710→## Security considerations
  1711→  1711→
  1712→  1712→### Disclaimer
  1713→  1713→
  1714→  1714→Hooks run with your system user's full permissions.
  1715→  1715→
  1716→  1716→<Warning>
  1717→  1717→  Hooks execute shell commands with your full user permissions. They can modify, delete, or access any files your user account can access. Review and test all hook commands before adding them to your configuration.
  1718→  1718→</Warning>
  1719→  1719→
  1720→  1720→### Security best practices
  1721→  1721→
  1722→  1722→Keep these practices in mind when writing hooks:
  1723→  1723→
  1724→  1724→* **Validate and sanitize inputs**: never trust input data blindly
  1725→  1725→* **Always quote shell variables**: use `"$VAR"` not `$VAR`
  1726→  1726→* **Block path traversal**: check for `..` in file paths
  1727→  1727→* **Use absolute paths**: specify full paths for scripts, using `"$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR"` for the project root
  1728→  1728→* **Skip sensitive files**: avoid `.env`, `.git/`, keys, etc.
  1729→  1729→
  1730→  1730→## Debug hooks
  1731→  1731→
  1732→  1732→Run `claude --debug` to see hook execution details, including which hooks matched, their exit codes, and output. Toggle verbose mode with `Ctrl+O` to see hook progress in the transcript.
  1733→  1733→
  1734→  1734→```

  1738→  1738→[DEBUG] Matched 1 hooks for query "Write"

content.text:
Perfect! I now have all the detailed information you need. Let me compile the exact specifications from the official documentation.

## Exact Hooks Configuration Format for Claude Code

Based on the official Claude Code documentation, here are the precise details you requested:

### 1. Exact JSON Structure for Defining Hooks

The hooks configuration follows this exact three-level nested structure in `.claude/settings.json`:

```json
{
  "hooks": {
    "EventName": [
      {
        "matcher": "regex_pattern_or_empty_string",
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command",
            "command": "shell_command_here",
            "async": false,
            "timeout": 600,
            "statusMessage": "optional_message"
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

**Exact field names:**
- Top level: `"hooks"`
- Event level: Event name like `"PreToolUse"`, `"Stop"`, `"SessionStart"`, etc.
- Matcher group level: `"matcher"` (regex string), `"hooks"` (array)
- Hook handler level (inside `hooks` array):
  - `"type"` (required): `"command"`, `"prompt"`, or `"agent"`
  - `"command"` (required for command hooks): shell command string
  - `"prompt"` (required for prompt/agent hooks): prompt text
  - `"async"` (optional, command hooks only): boolean, default `false`
  - `"timeout"` (optional): seconds, defaults: 600 for command, 30 for prompt, 60 for agent
  - `"statusMessage"` (optional): custom spinner message
  - `"model"` (optional, prompt/agent hooks): model override
  - `"once"` (optional, skills only): boolean

---

### 2. Exact Data Passed to Hook Scripts on stdin

Each event receives JSON on stdin with this structure. All events include these **common input fields**:

```json
{
  "session_id": "string",
  "transcript_path": "/absolute/path/to/transcript.jsonl",
  "cwd": "/current/working/directory",
  "permission_mode": "default|plan|acceptEdits|dontAsk|bypassPermissions",
  "hook_event_name": "EventName"
}
```

**Event-specific additions:**

**UserPromptSubmit:**
```json
{
  "prompt": "the user's submitted prompt text"
}
```

**PreToolUse:**
```json
{
  "tool_name": "Bash|Edit|Write|Read|Glob|Grep|WebFetch|WebSearch|Task|mcp__...",
  "tool_input": {
    "command": "npm test"  // varies by tool
  },
  "tool_use_id": "toolu_01ABC123..."
}
```

**PostToolUse:**
```json
{
  "tool_name": "string",
  "tool_input": { /* tool-specific */ },
  "tool_response": { /* tool-specific */ },
  "tool_use_id": "toolu_01ABC123..."
}
```

**Stop:**
```json
{
  "stop_hook_active": true|false,
  "last_assistant_message": "full text of Claude's last response"
}
```

**Notification:**
```json
{
  "message": "notification text",
  "title": "optional title",
  "notification_type": "permission_prompt|idle_prompt|auth_success|elicitation_dialog"
}
```

**SessionStart:**
```json
{
  "source": "startup|resume|clear|compact",
  "model": "claude-sonnet-4-6",
  "agent_type": "optional agent name if --agent flag used"
}
```

**SubagentStart:**
```json
{
  "agent_id": "agent-abc123",
  "agent_type": "Bash|Explore|Plan|custom_agent_name"
}
```

**SubagentStop:**
```json
{
  "stop_hook_active": true|false,
  "agent_id": "agent-abc123",
  "agent_type": "agent type name",
  "agent_transcript_path": "~/.claude/projects/.../abc123/subagents/agent-def456.jsonl",
  "last_assistant_message": "agent's final response text"
}
```

**ConfigChange:**
```json
{
  "source": "user_settings|project_settings|local_settings|policy_settings|skills",
  "file_path": "/optional/path/to/file"
}
```

**SessionEnd:**
```json
{
  "reason": "clear|logout|prompt_input_exit|bypass_permissions_disabled|other"
}
```

---

### 3. Environment Variables Available Inside Hooks

The following environment variables are available to all hook scripts:

- **`$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR`**: The project root directory (use in quotes: `"$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR"`)
- **`${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}`**: Plugin root directory (for plugin hooks only)
- **`$CLAUDE_CODE_REMOTE`**: Set to `"true"` in web environments, not set in local CLI
- **`$CLAUDE_ENV_FILE`**: Path to a file where you can persist environment variables (SessionStart hooks only). Write `export VAR=value` lines to this file and they'll be available in subsequent Bash commands.

No `CLAUDE_SESSION_ID` environment variable exists—the session ID comes via the `session_id` JSON field in hook input.

---

### 4. Event Names: SessionStart and SessionEnd Both Exist

Yes, **both exist**:

- **`SessionStart`**: Runs when a session begins or resumes (source: `"startup"`, `"resume"`, `"clear"`, or `"compact"`)
- **`SessionEnd`**: Runs when a session terminates (reason: `"clear"`, `"logout"`, `"prompt_input_exit"`, `"bypass_permissions_disabled"`, or `"other"`)

**Full list of 18 hook events:**
1. `SessionStart`
2. `UserPromptSubmit`
3. `PreToolUse`
4. `PermissionRequest`
5. `PostToolUse`
6. `PostToolUseFailure`
7. `Notification`
8. `SubagentStart`
9. `SubagentStop`
10. `Stop`
11. `TeammateIdle`
12. `TaskCompleted`
13. `ConfigChange`
14. `WorktreeCreate`
15. `WorktreeRemove`
16. `PreCompact`
17. `SessionEnd`

---

### 5. Stop Event: What's Included

The `Stop` event input includes:

```json
{
  "session_id": "abc123",
  "transcript_path": "/path/to/transcript.jsonl",
  "cwd": "/working/dir",
  "permission_mode": "default",
  "hook_event_name": "Stop",
  "stop_hook_active": true|false,
  "last_assistant_message": "full text of Claude's final response"
}
```

**Important**: 
- The `Stop` event includes **only the text content** of Claude's last response in `last_assistant_message`
- It does **NOT** include thinking blocks (extended thinking) directly in the hook input
- It does **NOT** include the full response object or thinking blocks—only the final text message
- To access thinking blocks or full response data, you must parse the transcript file at `transcript_path`

**Note on Stop hook loops**: The `stop_hook_active` field is `true` when Claude Code is already continuing as a result of a previous Stop hook. Always check this value to prevent infinite loops:

```bash
if [ "$(echo "$INPUT" | jq -r '.stop_hook_active')" = "true" ]; then
  exit 0  # Allow Claude to stop
fi
```

---

### Summary

This is the complete, exact specification from the official documentation. The configuration format is strict JSON with specific field names, event types support specific matcher patterns and decision control options, and hooks receive JSON context on stdin without direct access to thinking blocks (those require parsing the transcript file yourself).

input.content:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Claude Code session audit logger.

Copies the full session transcript (including thinking blocks, prompts,
tool use, and responses) to session_audits/ on every Stop and SessionEnd event.

Transcript format: JSONL (one JSON object per line) — Claude Code's native format.
"""

import sys
import json
import os
import shutil
from datetime import datetime


def main():
    project_dir = os.environ.get("CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR", os.getcwd())
    audit_dir = os.path.join(project_dir, "session_audits")
    os.makedirs(audit_dir, exist_ok=True)

    try:
        data = json.load(sys.stdin)
    except (json.JSONDecodeError, EOFError):
        return

    event = data.get("hook_event_name", "")
    session_id = data.get("session_id", "unknown")
    transcript_path = data.get("transcript_path", "")

    # Avoid Stop hook infinite loops
    if event == "Stop" and data.get("stop_hook_active"):
        return

    # Marker file tracks when this session started (for stable filenames)
    marker = os.path.join(audit_dir, f".{session_id}.start")
    short_id = session_id[:8] if len(session_id) > 8 else session_id

    if event == "SessionStart":
        ts = datetime.now().strftime("%Y-%m-%d_%H-%M-%S")
        with open(marker, "w") as f:
            f.write(ts)
        # Write a small initial metadata file
        out = os.path.join(audit_dir, f"{ts}_{short_id}.jsonl")
        with open(out, "w") as f:
            f.write(json.dumps({
                "event": "session_start",
                "timestamp": ts,
                "session_id": session_id,
                "source": data.get("source", "unknown"),
                "model": data.get("model", "unknown"),
            }) + "\n")
        return

    # Resolve the stable filename for this session
    if os.path.exists(marker):
        with open(marker) as f:
            ts = f.read().strip()
    else:
        # No marker yet (hook added mid-session) — create one now
        ts = datetime.now().strftime("%Y-%m-%d_%H-%M-%S")
        with open(marker, "w") as f:
            f.write(ts)

    out = os.path.join(audit_dir, f"{ts}_{short_id}.jsonl")

    # Copy the full transcript on Stop or SessionEnd
    if transcript_path and os.path.exists(transcript_path):
        shutil.copy2(transcript_path, out)

    # Clean up marker on SessionEnd
    if event == "SessionEnd":
        try:
            os.remove(marker)
        except OSError:
            pass


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

content.content: File created successfully at: C:\Users\flori\Code\AI for Interactive Media (Presentation)\.claude\hooks\audit-session.py

"""
Claude Code session audit logger.

Copies the full session transcript (including thinking blocks, prompts,
tool use, and responses) to session_audits/ on every Stop and SessionEnd event.

Transcript format: JSONL (one JSON object per line) — Claude Code's native format.
"""

import sys
import json
import os
import shutil
from datetime import datetime


def main():
    project_dir = os.environ.get("CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR", os.getcwd())
    audit_dir = os.path.join(project_dir, "session_audits")
    os.makedirs(audit_dir, exist_ok=True)

    try:
        data = json.load(sys.stdin)
    except (json.JSONDecodeError, EOFError):
        return

    event = data.get("hook_event_name", "")
    session_id = data.get("session_id", "unknown")
    transcript_path = data.get("transcript_path", "")

    # Avoid Stop hook infinite loops
    if event == "Stop" and data.get("stop_hook_active"):
        return

    # Marker file tracks when this session started (for stable filenames)
    marker = os.path.join(audit_dir, f".{session_id}.start")
    short_id = session_id[:8] if len(session_id) > 8 else session_id

    if event == "SessionStart":
        ts = datetime.now().strftime("%Y-%m-%d_%H-%M-%S")
        with open(marker, "w") as f:
            f.write(ts)
        # Write a small initial metadata file
        out = os.path.join(audit_dir, f"{ts}_{short_id}.jsonl")
        with open(out, "w") as f:
            f.write(json.dumps({
                "event": "session_start",
                "timestamp": ts,
                "session_id": session_id,
                "source": data.get("source", "unknown"),
                "model": data.get("model", "unknown"),
            }) + "\n")
        return

    # Resolve the stable filename for this session
    if os.path.exists(marker):
        with open(marker) as f:
            ts = f.read().strip()
    else:
        # No marker yet (hook added mid-session) — create one now
        ts = datetime.now().strftime("%Y-%m-%d_%H-%M-%S")
        with open(marker, "w") as f:
            f.write(ts)

    out = os.path.join(audit_dir, f"{ts}_{short_id}.jsonl")

    # Copy the full transcript on Stop or SessionEnd
    if transcript_path and os.path.exists(transcript_path):
        shutil.copy2(transcript_path, out)

    # Clean up marker on SessionEnd
    if event == "SessionEnd":
        try:
            os.remove(marker)
        except OSError:
            pass


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

# CLAUDE.md

## Project Conventions

- **Commit tagging**: Claude's commits use standard messages. Human commits should be identifiable — if unclear, Claude will ask.
- **Documentation**: Keep all project docs (`project_info.md`, `technical_info.md`, `design_guide.md`, `key_decisions.md`, `key_learnings.md`) up to date with every significant change.
- **Commit frequency**: Commit frequently in meaningful batches after changes.

---

## Contribution Log

content.text:
Done. Here's how it works:

**How it captures data:**
- Uses Claude Code's `transcript_path` — a JSONL file that Claude Code maintains internally with **everything**: your prompts, Claude's responses, thinking/reasoning blocks, all tool calls and results
- On every `Stop` event (after each Claude response) and `SessionEnd` (when you `/clear` or exit), the full transcript is copied to `session_audits/`

**File naming:** `session_audits/2026-02-26_14-30-00_a1b2c3d4.jsonl` — timestamp of session start + short session ID

**What's in each file:** Structured JSONL — one JSON object per line. You can later parse for:
- User messages (your prompts)
- Assistant messages (Claude's responses + thinking blocks)
- Tool use (every Read, Edit, Bash, etc. with inputs and outputs)

**Automatic:** Hooks fire on every session in this project — no manual steps needed.

**Note:** The hooks are now active. On your next `/clear` or session restart, the current session's transcript will be saved. You should see files appear in `session_audits/` going forward.

message.content: I presume: if I close the terminal window I'll 'loose' the session?

content.thinking:
The user is asking whether closing the terminal window would lose the session audit data. Let me think about this:

1. The `SessionEnd` hook fires on graceful exits (clear, logout, prompt_input_exit)
2. If you just close the terminal window, that's an abrupt termination - the `SessionEnd` hook might NOT fire
3. However, the `Stop` hook fires after EVERY Claude response - so the transcript gets copied after each response

So the answer is: you won't lose much, because the `Stop` hook copies the transcript after every response. You'd only lose whatever happened after the last Claude response (which would be nothing, since you can only close the terminal while Claude isn't responding, or mid-response).


The `Stop` hook captures the transcript after each of my responses, so closing the terminal abruptly would only miss the `SessionEnd` metadata—the actual conversation data is already safely copied.

content.text:
Practically, no. The `Stop` hook fires **after every Claude response**, so the transcript gets copied to `session_audits/` each time I finish speaking. By the time you're reading my answer and could close the terminal, the latest copy is already saved.

What you'd miss if you hard-close the terminal is only the `SessionEnd` event (which just cleans up a temp marker file — no data loss).

So the only scenario where you'd lose data is if you close the terminal **while Claude is mid-response** — that partial response wouldn't be captured. But everything up to the previous complete response would already be saved.

message.content: I the transcript appended or written in its entirety all the time?

content.text:
Written in its entirety every time — the script does `shutil.copy2()` of the full transcript file on each `Stop` event, overwriting the previous copy.

This means it's safe (no risk of corrupted appends), but also means it rewrites the entire file after every response. For a long session that could get large, but for typical presentation-building sessions it should be fine.

If you'd prefer an **append-only** approach (only writing new events since last copy), I can rework it — but the tradeoff is more complexity and a small risk of gaps if something goes wrong mid-session. The full-copy approach is simpler and guarantees completeness.

message.content: Where is the original file stored? within the project directory or somewhere global?

content.text:
Somewhere global — in `~/.claude/` (i.e., `C:\Users\flori\.claude\`). The `transcript_path` that Claude Code passes to the hook points to a file in its own internal session storage, not in your project.

So the audit hook is essentially copying from that global location into your project's `session_audits/` folder, which is why it's useful — otherwise the transcript would just live buried in Claude's internal directories and wouldn't be in your git history.

message.content: I'd say only copy it into the audit log after each of your responses conclude - instead of like, every time you append to the global transcript??

content.text:
That's already what it does — the `Stop` hook fires once after each complete Claude response, not on every internal transcript write. So it copies once per response turn, not continuously.

The flow is:
1. You send a prompt
2. Claude works (thinking, tool calls, writing)
3. Claude's full turn finishes → `Stop` hook fires → transcript copied
4. You're back at the prompt

One copy per turn.
